metadata.seed: Usually. Because I don't want them to think like, they shouldn't have had me because I'm so worthless to them.

patient.query: Usually. Because I don't want them to think like, they shouldn't have had me because I'm so worthless to them.

automatic_thoughts: they shouldn't have had me
I'm so worthless to them

neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re worried that others see you as a mistake or feel you’re not valued. What moments have you noticed where they’ve shown care or appreciation for you? How might you view those moments differently, or what other evidence could suggest they do value you?

patient.query: I guess I often focus on the times when I feel overlooked, like when I don’t get recognized for my work. It’s hard to remember the compliments or encouragements, even if they’re there. I think I just… I don’t know, I struggle to believe that anyone really means it when they say nice things about me.

automatic_thoughts: I feel overlooked
I struggle to believe compliments are genuine

contradiction: Tends to appear indifferent to praise or criticism (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re feeling overlooked and that compliments don’t feel genuine. What evidence do you have that people aren’t sincere? Can you think of a time when a compliment felt real to you?

patient.query: I mean, I guess I don’t have a lot of concrete evidence. It’s more of a feeling, you know? Like maybe I just assume people are being polite, not really seeing me. But there was one time, I remember a coworker said I did a great job on a project. I felt good in that moment, but then right after, I thought maybe they were just trying to be nice because they had to. It’s confusing.

automatic_thoughts: people are being polite, not really seeing me
they were just trying to be nice because they had to

entailment: Ability to recognize familiar people (observable entity)
neutral: Character trait finding of emotional warmth (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re feeling unsure about how others see you, especially after that compliment. It might help to think about other times when people have genuinely acknowledged your work—what did those moments look like? And what might be some reasons the coworker’s comment was sincere rather than obligatory?

patient.query: Yeah, it’s tough to look back and really see those moments clearly. I guess there have been a few times when I felt appreciated, but I just brush them off. Maybe because I think, "Well, they don’t know the whole me." It’s like I can’t shake this belief that I’m not enough, so I end up doubting good feedback. I don’t know, it just feels easier to assume they’re not being genuine.

automatic_thoughts: They don’t know the whole me
I’m not enough
Assume they’re not being genuine

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re holding a strong belief that you’re not enough, which makes it hard to accept praise. What evidence do you have that people truly know you, or that they might be offering genuine appreciation? How might you view a compliment differently if you considered that it could reflect something you’ve done well, even if it doesn’t capture every part of who you are?

patient.query: I guess I haven’t thought about it that way before. I mean, if I really looked at it, there have been times when I’ve worked hard and put in my best effort. Maybe they do see that part of me, even if it’s just a glimpse. But it’s still hard to shake the feeling that there’s more to me that they don’t see, and I end up feeling like I’m hiding. It’s like I keep those parts tucked away, and then I wonder

automatic_thoughts: They don't see the real me
I'm hiding parts of myself
Maybe they do see my effort

entailment: Ability to understand own emotions (observable entity)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re noticing a gap between how you see yourself and how you think others see you. What evidence do you have that they might not be seeing the parts of you that feel important? And can you recall any moments when someone did notice something you hadn’t expected?

patient.query: I guess I haven’t thought about it that way before. If I really looked at it, there have been times when I’ve worked hard and put in my best effort. Maybe they do see that part of me, even if it’s just a glimpse. But it’s still hard to shake the feeling that there’s more to me that they don’t see, and I end up feeling like I’m hiding. llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re feeling that your full self isn’t being recognized, even though you’ve noticed moments when people seem to notice your effort. What kinds of situations or comments have made you feel seen, even if only a glimpse? And what evidence might suggest that others are noticing more of you than you think?

patient.query: Yeah, I think that’s right. I mean, there have been times when I receive positive feedback on my presentations, or when a friend tells me they appreciate my support. In those moments, I laugh it off, like, "Oh, it was nothing." But I guess those comments show that they see me as someone who contributes. Still, I can’t help but think, “But what if they’re just saying that?” It’s like I can’t let myself fully accept it.

automatic_thoughts: But what if they’re just saying that?
I can’t let myself fully accept it.

neutral: Tends to be sensitive to criticism (finding)
contradiction: Tends to appear indifferent to praise or criticism (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re uncertain whether the compliments you receive are truly heartfelt, which makes you laugh them off. What signs or experiences suggest that people might be insincere? And what signs might indicate they genuinely value your work? You might find it helpful to weigh both sides before deciding how to respond.

patient.query: I suppose I just... tend to focus on situations where I felt ignored or dismissed in the past. Like, I remember a time when I really needed support, and nobody noticed. It makes me think that if they didn’t see me then, why would they see me now? On the flip side, when someone takes the time to talk about specifics in my work or mentions how I helped them, it feels different. But then I still doubt it—like, is it really about me or just

triggers: someone talks about specifics in my work
automatic_thoughts: if they didn’t see me then, why would they see me now?
is it really about me or just

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re carrying a lot of past experiences of feeling overlooked, and that can make it hard to trust when someone finally does notice you. What kinds of moments have you noticed that people do respond to you, and how do those moments feel different from the times you felt ignored? When you wonder if the attention is truly about you, what evidence or patterns do you see that might support or challenge that thought?

patient.query: I guess... when I think about it, the moments that stand out are often when I'm genuinely surprised, like when a colleague brings up something specific I contributed. I feel this rush of warmth in those moments, like someone really sees what I do. But then, there’s that nagging voice that says it’s just brief recognition or that they’re being nice. I guess that uncertainty dims the glow of those good moments, making me question their sincerity. It’s exhausting, honestly. I just wish

automatic_thoughts: it's just brief recognition
they're being nice
questioning their sincerity

neutral: Character trait finding of emotional warmth (finding)
Character trait finding of effort at work (finding)

llm_response.response: What evidence do you have that the recognition was only brief or insincere? Can you recall moments when the acknowledgment felt genuinely meaningful? How might your perspective shift if you considered that your colleague might truly value your contribution?

patient.query:
I don’t know... I mean, I guess I just tend to remember the times when I’ve felt like I didn’t measure up or when others have overlooked me. It kind of creates this lens where I assume that any recognition is just surface-level. But yeah, there have been those moments when a colleague really took the time to explain why they appreciated my input, and it felt good, like being seen. 

If I let myself believe that they genuinely value me, maybe it wouldn’t feel

triggers: times when I’ve felt like I didn’t measure up
moments when a colleague appreciated my input
automatic_thoughts: any recognition is just surface-level
they genuinely value me

entailment: Ability to recognize other persons (observable entity)

llm_response.response: It sounds like when you feel overlooked, you automatically assume any praise is just a surface gesture. Yet you also remember a colleague who took time to explain why they valued your input, which felt real. What evidence do you have that recognition can be both genuine and superficial? How might you view those moments differently if you allowed yourself to believe that people can truly value you?
